institution_affiliations.notes: Graduated magna cum laude. Met future husband Elliot Richardson there. Won Helen Homans Gilbert Award from Radcliffe.

narrative_blocks.body: As chairwoman of Reading Is Fundamental from 1981 to 1996, she grew the children served from three million to 3.7 million and the volunteer corps from 100,000 past 219,000. Radcliffe gave her its Gilbert Award; Lawrence University an honorary doctorate.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife of the Saturday Night Massacre
narrative_blocks.detail: Radcliffe College magna cum laude. Met Elliot Richardson at a Radcliffe dance: "he made a pest of himself by constantly cutting in, but she put up with it because he was a superb dancer" (Washington Post). When Nixon ordered Richardson to fire Archibald Cox: "There was never any doubt about Elliot's resignation... he knew he had no choice." Reading Is Fundamental: joined board 1969; chairwoman 1981-1996. Under her leadership, children served grew from 3M to 3.7M; volunteers from 100,000 to 219,000; federal funding from $7.6M to $13.2M. National Parnassus Award; Helen Homans Gilbert Award (Radcliffe). Also chairwoman, advisory board Second Genesis (DC drug treatment program) from 1978. Washington Post obituary Jul 28, 1999 (Bart Barnes). (Washington Post; Reading Is Fundamental; FindAGrave)
narrative_blocks.body: Anne Frances Hazard Richardson was the wife of Elliot Richardson, who resigned as Attorney General rather than fire Watergate prosecutor Archibald Cox in 1973. She was also the longtime chairwoman of Reading Is Fundamental, growing the program from 3M to 3.7M children served.

narrative_blocks.header: He was a pest, but he danced
narrative_blocks.body: She met Elliot Richardson when he kept cutting in at a Radcliffe dance her senior year — “she put up with it because he was a superb dancer.” Twenty-one years later, when Nixon ordered the Cox firing, “there was never any doubt about Elliot’s resignation.”

When Nixon ordered Richardson to fire Archibald Cox: "There was never any doubt about Elliot's resignation... he knew he had no choice." narrative_blocks.body: Anne Frances Hazard Richardson was the wife of Elliot Richardson, who resigned as Attorney General rather than fire Watergate prosecutor Archibald Cox in 1973. She was also the longtime chairwoman of Reading Is Fundamental, growing the program from 3M to 3.7M children served.

narrative_blocks.header: He was a pest, but he danced
narrative_blocks.body: She met Elliot Richardson when he kept cutting in at a Radcliffe dance her senior year — “she put up with it because he was a superb dancer.” Twenty-one years later, when Nixon ordered the Cox firing, “there was never any doubt about Elliot’s resignation.”

narrative_blocks.header: Resigned 1973 rather than fire Watergate prosecutor
narrative_blocks.body: On 20 October 1973 he resigned as US Attorney General rather than obey President Nixon's order to fire Watergate special prosecutor Archibald Cox. His resignation — along with Deputy AG William Ruckelshaus's simultaneous resignation — precipitated the constitutional crisis that became known as the Saturday Night Massacre.

narrative_blocks.header: Combat medic crossed minefield at Utah Beach, 1944
narrative_blocks.body: He served as combat medic with the US 4th Infantry Division at the D-Day landing on 6 June 1944. He crossed a minefield to rescue a fellow officer and was among the first up Causeway Number 2 from Utah Beach — earning the Bronze Star and a Purple Heart with oak leaf cluster.

narrative_blocks.header: Four US Cabinet positions, matched only by Shultz
narrative_blocks.body: He held four US Cabinet positions: Secretary of HEW 1970-73, Secretary of Defense Jan-May 1973, Attorney General May-Oct 1973, and Secretary of Commerce 1976-77. Only George Shultz has matched the four-Cabinet record in American history.

narrative_blocks.header: Clerked for Learned Hand and Felix Frankfurter, Harvard
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard Law School in 1947 — where he was president of the Harvard Law Review — he clerked for Judge Learned Hand of the 2nd Circuit and then for Justice Felix Frankfurter at the Supreme Court. The two clerkships placed him at the apex of mid-century American legal training.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the family name of his mother Mary Pierrepont Bushnell Hazard (HD1577).

narrative_blocks.detail: St. George's School Newport RI; Yale B.A. 1915 (Wolf's Head). WWI service included Mexican border intervention. Solvay Process Company 1915-1923 (rising to assistant to the president). RI State Senator 1930-1932; RI State Treasurer 1938-1940. WWII: Chief, New England War Production Board priorities section 1941-1943; Civil Affairs Officer in Tunisia, Palermo, and Naples; Liaison Officer to Chief Quartermaster, US Army — played a major role in food distribution to liberated northwestern Europe. Discharged Lt. Colonel November 1945. Published "Civil Affairs: An Experiment in Reconstruction" (1949). His papers (20 linear feet, 1893-1979) are at the Rhode Island Historical Society (RIHS MSS 483 sg 9). (Rhode Island Historical Society; Yale; FindAGrave)
narrative_blocks.body: Served as Rhode Island State Treasurer 1938-1940; ran as Republican nominee for US Senate 1948 (lost to Theodore F. Green). The 1948 race was the high-water mark of his political career after South Kingstown Town Council, RI State Senate, and an unsuccessful 1932 US House run.

As Liaison Officer to the Chief Quartermaster of the U.S. Army based in England from February 1944, he played a major role in food distribution to liberated northwestern Europe. Discharged Lt. Colonel November 1945 after Tunisia, Palermo, and Naples Civil Affairs assignments.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote a 1949 book on civil affairs reconstruction
narrative_blocks.body: Published 'Civil Affairs: An Experiment in Reconstruction' (1949) based on his WWII Liaison Officer experience. Also co-authored a 1951 American Fisheries Society paper on modification of the sexual cycle in brook trout — his family's American Fish Culture Company connection.

He enlisted Troop D 1st NY Cavalry as a private in October 1915 at twenty-three; called to active duty for the Mexican border intervention June 1916; commissioned 2nd Lt. then promoted 1st Lt. in the 14th U.S. Cavalry at Del Rio TX. Discharged February 1919 — never went overseas in WWI.

education.notes: — Wolf's Head Senior Society. Psi Upsilon Fraternity.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the Pierpont branch through mother Mary Elizabeth Blake's ancestry (H00388 → HD1556 → HD1565).

INST039.primary_name: Radcliffe College (now Radcliffe Institute, Harvard University)
INST039.name: Radcliffe College (now Radcliffe Institute, Harvard University)
INST039.notes: Founded 1879, Cambridge MA. Women's coordinate college of Harvard. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Anne Frances Hazard Richardson (HD1587), daughter of Thomas Pierrepont Hazard (HD1579), graduated magna cum laude. She later won the Helen Homans Gilbert Award from Radcliffe for her work chairing Reading Is Fundamental 1981-1996. (HD1587 notes; Washington Post obituary Jul 28, 1999)
